speaker: interviewer
text: --Anger. Did the everyday life change at the Swedish delegation after Raoul Wallenberg arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, of course, we-- we had to put everything aside, just for doing one thing, namely to support and help the Raoul Wallenberg mission. So everyone was, more or less-- the girls were typing, writing, sending code telegrams to Sweden and all that and so on. And-- and we-- we were helping to negotiate with the-- the Hungarian government about these passports. We had a supporting role in that-- on that respect, and-- and so on. So I would say that the whole-- the whole staff were, more or less, engaged in this work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the-- this passport, this Schutz-Pass came from? Did it came from Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they were printed in Budapest. We had a big operation there. Of course, it was sad enough that even other people managed to illegally print this. So there were, towards the end of the war, many, many false passports circulating, printed by some organizations who I don't know really, really who they were. But that was a fact. And they came in hands of the Nazis, and also, which-- which, of course, sometimes they blame Raoul Wallenberg for having given the Nazi people their passports, which, of course, never happened. But-- but this-- this is-- was a very, very difficult situation, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these people making money by selling the passports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember any tragical incident during your stay with Raoul Wallenberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I told you, I-- I stayed in the house owned by Manfred Weiss, a Jewish industrialist. He and his family then, later on, managed to-- to buy themselves out from-- That's the only group I know of who managed to-- to escape with-- after negotiating with the Germans. But anyhow, that's beside the point, just now, because in this house was a distant relative of Manfred Weiss, also, and his wife living. So I had breakfast with them, every morning, there. And one day, they didn't turn up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started to wonder, where are they? That was just a-- a couple of days after the arrival of the-- the-- the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were rumors already going around the city that the Germans, which proved to be right then, that they took key persons, like as hostages to go a little, whatever. But they were all-- all deported to-- to Auschwitz. Not that many, but a couple of hundred people, wealthy business people and all that, the key persons in the Jewish community, all of a sudden, just disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wondered, what has happened now to this-- this Mr. Weiss, who stayed with us in the same house? So I started to-- to-- to-- to inquire. And then I heard that there was a camp just-- not far away from the embassy, kind of a first stage or provisional sort of camp, with-- fenced in like that. So I went there to have a look. And then I saw, inside this place, people walking around. And all of a sudden, I saw this man, Weiss, coming. He's down there. So I said-- And he came towards me, and standing on the other side of the fence, making this gesture, only to say, well, what can I do? And he said, you can do absolutely nothing. He said, we are lost. We are doomed.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was already aware of this. And-- and then the following day, they were evacuated and-- and-- and sent to-- to-- to Auschwitz. Another episode was when a Swedish lady married to a Hungarian Jew-- she was in Sweden. And we got instructions from the Foreign Office to try to save her son, who was staying in the south of-- of Hungary, in-- in-- down at the Pecs. This is very far away. A whole-- it took me the whole day to go. Because I-- I-- I-- my boss for permission to go and see if I could save that boy. And I came to-- to that house where he was staying with his-- his grandparents. And I said, I'm so and so. And I have the instructions from the Foreign Office and from his mother, who has asked us to try to save the little boy here. And she said-- they said, oh, yes. That, we understand. And you have to ask for permission from the father. And he's in a labor camp outside. Many-- not many but a few of the Jewish people, who were taken to labor camps to work, they-- they saved themselves. They were saved, in a way, because they were never deported to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was one of them. So I went there. And of course, he very gladly gave his permission for me to take the boy with me. So I took the boy with me, took away this yellow star and all that. And-- and through my connections then, in the-- in the-- in the German embassy, I said, here's a Swedish boy. Please give him a visa through Germany. So he got a visa. And I sent him home by plane. And he arrived safely in Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: The following day, the whole village was evacuated and sent to Auschwitz. So I just came in the last, very last minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Ragnar, the little boy, Ragnar. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a Swedish Name, wasn't it

speaker: interviewee
text: I have forgotten his family name just  now. Anyhow, so that was him. Then, the next experience I had was I had heard that outside, just outside Budapest, they had, in a-- in a factory, on the compound of a factory there, collected thousands of Jewish people, who were standing or sitting like under-- in the open air, a whole week without food or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in a miserable condition, of course. So I said, well, why don't I try to see how that is? So I went there. And I found then the whole place fenced in, like that, around the factory buildings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the-- on the place there, there were all these Jews lying there or standing or-- or-- or waiting for something. And that something was a train coming in. And the-- the door were opened-- was opened. And the people were pushed into these cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw how one soldier took a small boy, by his leg, and threw him in like that, into the car, you know? And-- and when they have packed that car completely, they put in a bucket of water just to show how-- this humanitarian gesture. And then they closed-- they nailed the-- the doors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then that train, as I heard afterwards, was circulating a whole week in-- in Buda-- in Hungary till they were transferred over to Auschwitz, when the-- a big part of these-- of the Jewish people were-- were dead or got insane or, you know, all that. It was the most-- most barbaric, most terrible way of-- of deporting people, as I thought then. I would have experience of another, which was, perhaps, the same cruel method but in another way. And that was when I was with Raoul Wallenberg, now and then, at these so-called death marches from Budapest to-- to Hegyeshalom. That is the-- the border city between Austria and-- and Hungary. And that was towards the end of the war, when when the transport line, the trail-- the-- the trains didn't function any longer. Either it was bombed, and so on. So in their desperation then, to get the people deported, the Nazis-- that was the-- the Hungarian Nazis, who were active then, at that time. They had taken over, you know, as I told you, at the end, towards the end, after the 15th of October. They pushed the people. They took the people, as they were, standing in their ordinary clothes, perhaps in the streets or whatever. They just took them all. Took them out from houses and pushed them to march these 200 kilometers in the snow. It took a week, that march to-- to the border. Where then they were taken over by-- by the German Eichmann commando and sent to Auschwitz. But that was a very barbaric way of doing it, because they-- they had to spend the night in open air, in the cold. And-- and there were corpses all around. And they were dying during the way, during the march, and all that. And what we could do was very little. We tried to distribute food and all that as-- as much as we could. It was forbidden, of course. And Wallenberg managed, at the border town there, to save a few people also. Now and then, there are many of these episodes which I was a-- where I was a witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you went there by car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you only-- was it only one car or many cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- we had one car, together. And then we had trucks. And-- and when Wallenberg managed to-- he bribed them, these Nazi people there and so on. So now and then, we managed to get people back, if he could prove that they had Swedish passports and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could you bribe the Nazi? Was it with money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that-- that was Wallenberg's way of doing it. He-- he had the-- spoke to the commandant. And he gave him a-- a bottle of cognac or whatever, and so on, and got on speaking terms with him, and so on, and-- and said that, here are some people here with Swedish passports. And-- and how he-- how he negotiated that? He was very clever man in that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many could you save just that-- that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's, of course, a question how many we saved that way. But I would guess-- also, the Swiss, the Swiss embassy and all that followed our example. And I think we-- we managed, together with the Swiss, to save a couple of thousand people that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were there any special negotiation with the Nazi that they are not common knowledge? Did you have any special negotiation with the Nazis at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- and that is where I was involved, because it was with the Foreign Office. And that-- I felt free then to negotiate with them. And that is how this critical situation that came about when the-- the-- the Nyilas-- that's the Hungarian name of the Hungarian Nazis-- when they, all of a sudden, after the 15th of October then, when they took over, decided that these protective passports had-- had no value any longer. And that was, of course, a catastrophical situation for us. And that is how Wallenberg managed to, through the wife of the then foreign minister-- Kemeny was his name-- she approached. She was suffering very much of-- of seeing all this, because she was a-- a rather-- she was a very-- she was a humanitarian person. And she'd always objected against this-- these kind of methods and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: She approached Wallenberg and said, what can I do to help? And then Wallenberg told her that, if you cooperate now and convince your husband that he has to announce that, after all, these passport are still valid, I will do my best to see to it that your husband, after the war, won't be accused of being a-- a war criminal. That I-- I'll try to help safe him-- save him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, Raoul Wallenberg could never do that, because he was, himself, deported by the-- by the Soviets. And he couldn't help this-- this Kemeny was his name, Baron Kemeny.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, she-- she convinced her husband to go to the radio and declare these passports valid, which he did. And so for the moment, we managed, through-- through Raoul Wallenberg's ingenuity, to save the situation for a while. And what-- what date approximately was it? In what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it could have been-- it could have been a rather early stage. It could have been at the, say, at the end of-- at the end of October or something, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened during the very last day of the-- the fighting between the Germans and the Russian? Did-- did you-- did you see any fighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were on the-- we were bombed, of course, the whole time. We were-- I didn't-- I didn't see it, because we-- we were never part of a battle in the-- on the Buda side, between the Germans and the-- the Russian-- and the Soviets. I guess that Wallenberg, who stayed on-- on the Pest side, and those people there, that was the fighting, house by house, like that, how the-- the Soviets-- the Soviets' army entered the city, big, heavy fightings. On the Buda side, they-- they managed to get in without any fightings. So they were good. They-- they made a trap for the garrison in Buda to-- to try to get out. So they managed. They opened a kind of a-- a possibility there, in the-- in the-- when they encircled Buda. They-- they opened a-- a sort of a gate for the Germans, who said, 'we'll try this.' So they tried to evacuate and marched out. The whole army that were in Buda marched out and out of the city. And the Russians were just waiting for that. So when they have got them out, they killed them all by machine guns. So it was an easy matter for the Soviets then to enter the Buda side without any fightings at all. So that-- that saved-- that saved the-- the civilian population in Buda.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened with those Jews in the safe house at the very last moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the-- the-- the Nazi, the Hungarian Nazi tried to enter and take away. And they managed, in the-- in the very last days, to get out perhaps a couple of hundreds or whatever. And they took them to the Danube and shot them and throw them into the Danube. But we were grateful that the main part were-- were saved. The main part in these houses, of the population, who were in these-- in these houses were saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you any witness of this--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --killing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on the Buda side, so I didn't see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your first Russian soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was in-- in February, I think, the beginning of February. We thought that, why should we-- why should we stay on in Buda, with the Swiss, we and the neutral embassies, and be part of a bloodbath here, when the fighting will start? We have done our share. And we-- I-- I suggested we try to evacuate the embassies from that part. As I said, happily enough, that-- this situation never came about, which we have feared. But anyhow, I-- we tried to evacuate. And-- and we went out, a colleague of mine-- I'm describing that in my book. It's how we came to a house, on a-- another hill near-- nearby, where we had a house, at our disposal, for these purposes, to evacuate. And there we came, he and I. And-- and we-- when we came there, the Germans were withdrawing. And they started to dig their trenches in our garden. I was very upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told them, look here, this is Swedish property. And they just laughed at me and said, well, this is-- this is war, you know? So that's the-- there's nothing you can do to that. So we-- we went down into the shelter and had a-- had a sleep-- sort of to sleep. And I was awakened by-- by the-- the house-- housekeeper of the house, who came rushing down. There were-- there are soldiers in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is my first confrontation with the-- with the Russians. There are soldiers in the kitchen. And I said, now, I don't want to meet-- meet these Germans. And, no, they speak another language, she said. And then I came up in the kitchen. There was a-- a patrol of three Soviet elite soldiers, in-- dressed up in white anoraks, because it was snow and winter and all that, very neat, very correct. And I showed them my papers. And they said, OK. And they-- they left the house. So I got a very first-- a very good impression from-- by the Soviets. And there then came-- then, just after that, there was a knock on the door. And in came a Mongol-looking officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was it, the same day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same day, just one hour after. There came-- came the new--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wave?

speaker: interviewee
text: First came the elite troops, and then came the others, which were worse. And then, they-- they wanted to have the house, because they-- they thought this was a good house and all that. And I said, well, you can't take the house, because it's Swedish property and all that. Ah, they said. No, you have to-- you have to negotiate with the general then, they said then. So I sent my-- my colleague with him. And my colleague come rushing back, and this German-- this Soviet officer, with a pistol, after him, pointing at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my colleague said, look here, you have to give your watch. Because he want my watch. I have no watch just now. So I said to myself, why should I, a Swedish diplomat, give my watch to a Soviet officer? So but after having-- thinking it over for a second, I said to myself, well, if I don't give it, he will shoot me. And he will take the whole-- the watch anyhow. So that-- so I gave it to him, politely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was very, very happy. He-- he changed his view, completely. And he praised me for this gesture. And he lined up his soldiers, outside the-- the house, and said, this man, you know, he's a very fine man. And now you will defend this house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they defended this house from being plundered by the other Soviet troops coming afterwards. And so that is the beginning, how we got a contact with the-- with the Soviets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got-- we had the luck to get a contact with the KGB staff officers. And we were then interned and stayed with-- with the-- the Soviets for three months, where they unhappily not did communicate with the Swedes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Sweden, they thought that we were long dead and-- and all that. So my-- my-- my dear wife, staying in Sweden then, at the time, she was-- it wasn't very encouraging for her to-- not to know what had happened to us. Did-- did you know where Raoul Wallenberg was at the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, well, but we--

speaker: interviewer
text: --time of the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew. We knew that he was on the Pest side. We knew that he was going to contact the-- the Soviets. And-- and so we thought, at that time, that he was long in Sweden, that he had been sent back to Sweden long, long ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So not until we start our journey to Sweden, and we are on the way home, we learned that Raoul Wallenberg hasn't yet arrived in Sweden. So that was the first signs that-- that something had happened to him. Could you describe your travel from Budapest to the end, Stockholm in-- which--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your travel between Budapest at and Stockholm? How many-- how many days did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. Well, we-- we went first by bus. The first day, they-- it wasn't very long till they got instructions to send us home. But then we-- we-- they evacuated us, by bus, to Bucharest, and there, by train, from Bucharest to Odessa, Moscow, Helsinki, almost Stockholm. That was a-- a journey taking about one month or something like that. It was very slow. There was-- you know the-- Russia was very destroyed by the war. And the communication lines, it was-- they had to repair, very often, before we could continue with the train and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during that trip, we were treated in a correct way by-- by the-- by the Soviets. Did you have any contact with the KGB?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: At the beginning? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --at the beginning, yes. They-- they took care of us and so on. And then they had, with us, all the time, an officer, a KGB officer. Or-- or if he was an army officer, I don't know. But he was our-- accompanied us the whole way till the Finnish border. So we were under guard the whole way-- the whole way, of course. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you on the way to Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were the-- my-- my boss then, the ambassador, myself, and then two others. Lars Berg, he was the head of the-- he was section for the foreign interests, as I told you, with his next in command, called Carlsson. We had a Swede called Ekmark, who was in charge of all this buying foodstuffs for-- for the houses under our protection and all that and so on. We had the two girls, Asta Nilsson, who was the Save the Children representative, Save the Jewish Children. And-- and Margareta Bauer, who was the secretary at the embassy. And the chancery officer, Denes von Mezey, he was also with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so I think that's-- that was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with the embassy staff of the neutral country, like Switzerland? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the-- the staff of the Swiss embassy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Switzerland. Well, they were-- they were taken. And that was what I learned when I came home to Sweden. So my colleague there, he was a young man, like me. He and the-- the consul they had and-- and two-- and two others, about three or four employees by-- in the-- in the Swiss embassy were all taken, together, brought to-- to the prison, Lubyanka Prison in Moscow. I think that was the first prison they were taken to. I met him, of course, after the war, again. And he was back. And he said to me that, it was quite clear, from the beginning, that they were taken because the Soviets wanted them as kind of exchange objects in case they-- they would need to get back some of their people. And that was just what happened in Switzerland, when the Swiss had a-- a Soviet delegation or Soviet people who they had imprisoned in Switzerland. And they exchanged them for-- for the two diplomats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think-- do you think that they took Raoul for the same purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think, in a way, perhaps, yes. But the main reason for them to arrest Raoul Wallenberg was that they suspected him to be an agent, a spy, perhaps not only for the Americans. Because they knew that these connections we had with the Americans the whole time, you know, and the financial aid-- aid and all that we got. But also, when they found that he had meetings with Eichmann and all that, they said to themselves, oh, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: He must be a double agent, then. He was-- and-- and so on. And there comes into the picture, also, this situation where you ask yourself, how-- how-- how does it happen that the Germans allowed-- that the Germans allowed us to act as we did in front of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- as I told you the story, we liberated the Jewish people and so on. I think the answer to that is that they were very, very anxious not to violate the relationship, the diplomatic relationship with Sweden. They needed Sweden, up in the north, as a neutral country, as a breathing hole. And I also think that the-- Himmler and those-- and those people, already at that time was thinking of, perhaps, one day, we could use Sweden as an intermediary to get a peace treaty with the Allies, perhaps, behind the backs of the-- of the Soviets, as well. And of course, a very unrealistic idea, of course. But anyhow-- so he was sort of having that. He is buying all the-- So I'm-- I'm-- I'm quite sure that the Germans in Budapest had a kind of a standing instructions not to-- to-- to be very careful about this and not to violate the Swedish delegation, or the Swedish mission and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to cut now and change tape. Thank you.
